observation: I'm proxying requests through gopher and noticed CRLF sequences are tolerated inside the gopher URI without rejection

person_a_output.title: Gopher Protocol CRLF Tolerance
person_a_output.knowledge: Some HTTP client libraries construct gopher:// URIs by directly embedding user-controlled data without sanitizing CRLF sequences, because gopher is treated as a legacy protocol with relaxed validation compared to http/https.
trigger_condition: Observing that a gopher:// request is constructed from user input
Confirming CRLF sequences pass through unescaped into the gopher URI

general: HTTP client libraries with gopher support

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Legacy protocol handlers often receive less input validation than primary protocols.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [client supports a legacy/rarely-used protocol scheme] AND [CRLF is not stripped from that scheme's URI] THEN [arbitrary line injection into the underlying raw socket stream is possible]
generalizes_to: protocol smuggling via under-validated legacy schemes

pitfalls: Assuming all protocol handlers share the same sanitization as http/https
person_a_output.chain_potential: Enables raw protocol smuggling to non-HTTP services reachable via SSRF.

person_a_output.title: Gopher SSRF for Non-HTTP Services
person_a_output.knowledge: Once CRLF injection into a gopher URI is confirmed, the same SSRF primitive can be redirected at non-HTTP services (Redis, SMTP, Memcached) that interpret raw line-based protocols.
trigger_condition: Confirming CRLF injection into a gopher:// request (see ek_0002)
Identifying internal non-HTTP services reachable from the vulnerable host

general: internal service exploitation via protocol smuggling

abstracted_pattern.narrative: An SSRF primitive that allows raw line injection can be repurposed to speak arbitrary line-based protocols to internal services.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [SSRF supports gopher and CRLF injection] AND [internal line-based service is reachable] THEN [attacker can issue arbitrary commands to that internal service]
generalizes_to: internal service command injection via URL fetchers

pitfalls: Forgetting that some internal services require multi-step handshakes that gopher's single payload can't replicate
person_a_output.chain_potential: Direct continuation of ek_0002; can chain further into service-specific exploitation.
person_a_output.confidence_rationale: Demonstrated against Redis and SMTP in prior public write-ups.

person_a_output.title: Inconsistent Cross-Protocol Header Validation
person_a_output.knowledge: Backends sometimes validate headers differently depending on whether the request arrived over HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2, creating a discrepancy an attacker can exploit.
trigger_condition: Observing different header validation outcomes for the same payload depending on protocol version

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Validation logic tied to one protocol version may not be re-applied after a downgrade.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [request is downgraded across protocol versions] AND [validation is version-specific] THEN [a bypass may exist at the downgrade boundary]

pitfalls: Testing only one protocol version and missing the discrepancy
person_a_output.confidence_rationale: Observed in fewer real-world cases than ek_0002/ek_0003.

prev.title: RFC Violation Bypass via Leading Whitespace

person_d_final_response_shape.reasoning: Matched because the observation describes CRLF tolerance in a gopher URI, which is the exact trigger condition for ek_0002.
pitfalls: Assuming all protocol handlers share the same sanitization as http/https
